data.title: Absci: Still A Work In Progress, Fighting To Survive
data.description:
Investment Overview

In my last post on Absci (NASDAQ:ABSI) for Seeking Alpha, written in May last year, I was somewhat critical of companies using AI to try to develop new and better medicines, which is the Vancouver based company's core business.

I noted that the company raised ~$230m via its July 2021 IPO, selling 12.5m shares at $16, and that they had subsequently fallen in value to <$1.5, and also noted the disappointing performance of other AI drug discovery companies that had completed similarly large IPO's, writing:

AbCellera Biologics Inc. (ABCL), based in Vancouver like Absci, raised ~$550m at $20 per share in 2020. Schrödinger (SDGR) raised $232m at $17 per share in 2020. Certara, Inc. (CERT) raised ~$335m in 2020 at $23 per share. Recursion Pharmaceuticals, Inc. (RXRX) raised ~$500m in 2021, at $18 per share. Exscientia raised $510m at $22 per share in 2021.

Every company mentioned above bar Certara - down nearly 40% - is down >65% since IPO, with three companies' share prices down >75%. Over the same period, the S&P 500 (SP500) is +20%.

This remains the case today, although Exscientia has since become part of Recursion. In fairness, AbCellera stock is now up >65% on a 12 months basis, but still down nearly 80% since IPO.

I was critical of the thesis that if we build a big enough computer, and put enough data in it, it will figure out how to develop better drugs than are on the market today, noting that no successful drugs to date had been developed using AI.

That is a simplistic view - AI in the right hands can be a powerful tool - but my point that this has generally been a bad sector to be invested in, seems to hold true - Absci stock is down nearly 50% since I issued a Hold recommendation last year, concluding:

While the research seems valid and intriguing, were it not for the "AI" tag, Absci Corporation's value in the marketplace would likely not support a market cap valuation much above $250m, based on my experience researching non-AI based biotech companies and their pipeline.

Presently, Absci's share price is $2.53, and its market cap valuation is $378m. In this note, I'll update on developments at the company since my last note and speculate about the company's prospects for the next 12-15 months.

Absci - Catching Up On Developments, From May Last Year To Q2 2025 Earnings

One positive of holding the stock of an AI driven drug developer is that shares typically spike in reaction to any positive newsflow, as, for example, Absci's share price did in February this year, apparently in response to a press conference at the White House to unveil a $500bn AI initiative, the Stargate Project.

Unfortunately, the positive sentiment does not typically last and Absci's share price, after spiking in value to >$5 per share, was trading <$2.5 per share just a couple of weeks later. In July, shares also briefly spiked as the company reported a ~$50m fundraising completed at $3 per share. The company filed for a $400m mixed securities shelf in August this year also.

Reporting Q2 earnings, Absci recorded revenue of $0.6m, down from $1.3m in the prior year period, R&D expenses of $20.5m, and a net loss of $(30.6m), compared to $(24.8m) in Q2 2024. Cash position was reported as $117.5m

Before we consider Absci's current platform, let's remind ourselves of the company's approach, as per a business description included in the Q2 earnings release:

Our Integrated Drug Creation platform combines cutting-edge AI models with a synthetic biology data engine, enabling the rapid design of innovative therapeutics that address challenging therapeutic targets. Absci’s approach leverages a continuous feedback loop between advanced AI algorithms and wet lab validation. Each cycle refines our data and strengthens our models, facilitating rapid innovation and enhancing the precision of our therapeutic designs.

The below slide taken from a recent investor presentation also highlights some of the work the company has been engaged in, which is intriguing and impressive, in my view, if not yet lucrative in any way.

Now let's consider the pipeline:

As we can see, Absci's pipeline is immature, with only a single asset in a clinical study, ABS-101, indicated for inflammatory bowel disease and targeting the protein TL1A.

Some quick research online tells me that Pharma giants Sanofi (SNY), AbbVie (ABBV), Pfizer (PFE) and Merck (MRK) are all developing drugs that target TL1A, or Tumor Necrosis Factor-like Ligand 1A to give it its full name. Absci believes its drug may have a superior all-round profile, with the potential for once quarterly dosing, and has promised Phase 1 data this year, although it may not reveal much - a Phase 1/2a is slated to begin in early 2026, management says. The market opportunity is >$20bn, Absci believes, but the competition intense.

Meanwhile, ABS-201 targets another large market in androgenetic alopecia, which affects ~80m Americans, Absci says, and has not had a new drug approved since the 1990's. The target is the prolactin receptor ("PRLR"), which drives hair follicle regression, and studies in non-human primates showed that hair density and thickness improved over a short treatment duration. Absci has created a Scientific Advisory Board comprised of hair experts, and it ought to be intriguing to see how that program progresses, with Phase 1/2a studies scheduled for early 2026.

ABS-301 was discovered via Absci's reverse immunology platform, and again, the program looks interesting, with an innovative mechanism of action ("MoA"), as shown in the slide below:

Once again, target markets - a range of solid tumor cancers - run into the double-digit billions, while the final candidate, ABS-501, targets HER2 and is being designed as a more effective trastuzumab - Roche's (OTCQX:RHHBY), which earned ~$1.5bn of revenues last year.

In terms of partnerships, Absci's founder, CEO, President and Director Sean McClain told analyst's on the Q2 earnings call:

earlier this year, AMD - Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) - made a $20 million strategic investment in Absci, reflecting their conviction in the potential of our AI-driven drug creation platform. Our collaboration continues to advance and AMD compute solutions supporting key workloads across our antibody design platform. We will continue to share key updates to this strategic collaboration in the future as they occur.

AMD is a massive company with a market cap of $271bn, and investments of this nature are essentially the lifeblood of companies like Absci, given that the prospect of commercial revenues is still years away.

Absci has attracted staff from companies such as Nvidia (NVDA), Open AI, and Google, and Pharmas including Pfizer, AstraZeneca (AZN), and Novo Nordisk (NVO), and from a research perspective, the Broad Institute, all of which is encouraging in my view. The company has a 77k square foot "drug creation and wet lab space" in Vancouver, and an Innovation Centre in Zug, Switzerland, the headquarters of Crispr Therapeutics and an established biotech hub.

The company says it has been involved in 25-partnered programs to date, including Pharmas Merck and AstraZeneca, and worked on an intriguing project with Caltech focused on HIV, and on data programs with Nvidia and Oracle. A note in the company presentation states that the it anticipates "signing one or more partnerships, including with a Large Pharma in 2025".

Analysis - Intriguing Programs But No Value Proposition - Yet

In all honesty, since my last note I'd say Absci has made incremental, as opposed to rapid progress - there has not been much in the way of share price needle moving events or developments, which explains the company's declining share price.

This is the reality for most, if not all AI-driven drug developers in my view - breakthroughs in biotech are not easy to come by, and when building a drug candidate from scratch, researching the best targets, progress is only ever likely to be slow in the first few years - possibly even the first 10-15 years.

Does that make Absci uninvestable? Not necessarily. Preclinical and Phase 1 data, except in the rarest of circumstances, does not drive share price gains for biotechs, because the market is aware that on only ~10% of occasions does promising early data translate into a successful drug candidate. What's needed is compelling Phase 2 "proof-of-concept" data, and that is 18-24 months away for Absci, perhaps longer, and for a single drug candidate, or perhaps two - ABS-101 and ABS-201.

Absci needs to make sure it can survive financially - the company made a net loss of $(56.7m) in 1H25, and reported current assets of $137.4m, therefore investors should be aware there is a risk of the company running out of funding potentially before the end of next year, although my feeling is there is enough goodwill in the market for the company to be able raise further funding if necessary.

Investors in Absci need to acknowledge that this is a fledgling company, and the tangible risk that despite best efforts, long-term survival is not guaranteed. I'd speculate the company is simply in "survival" mode at this time, attempting to refine its technology and approach, and may need to develop and guide many more assets through the clinical study process before the market gets excited about a potentially commercially viable drug and buys in.

Attracting a "Big Pharma" partner or further investment from an Nvidia or an AMD would be a huge boon for the company, and the only way I see the share price picking up value in the near term - potentially a significant amount of value. Short-term investors may find that prospect a reason for buying stock, but ultimately, when investing in Absci, investors should be thinking about a 10-15 year horizon.

If Absci still exists as a company in 10-15 years time it's possible it may have become a commercial stage Pharma, developing a wide range of assets, and working with numerous partners, within an industry with very high barriers to entry. That is the bull case, the bear case being that the company is unable to attract enough investment in the short-to-medium term, and ends up delisting, or merging with another company, as Exscientia did.

Due to the risks involved, I remain on the side-lines, although having one or two AI-drug developers in your investment portfolio is not necessarily a bad idea, provided you are investing money you can afford to lose, because if this industry does eventually start "winning", it will likely "win big".

data.title: Lazard Equity Franchise Portfolio Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Overview

Global equity markets rose in the second quarter, in a volatile period marked by a steep sell-off after the rollout of US tariffs in early April, which was then followed by a recovery through May and June. US trade policy remained a focal point for investors, as negotiations between the US and its major trading partners continued amid hopes that the US will adopt a more accommodating stance on import taxes. The US stock market ended the quarter at a new record high despite uncertainty about tariffs and the Federal Reserve’s decision to hold interest rates steady because of it. The strong rally staged by European equity markets in the first two months of the quarter fizzled in June, with most major markets posting minor losses in local terms. Inflation numbers picked up in June, fueling speculation that the European Central Bank might pause its rate-cutting campaign.

Portfolio Review

In the second quarter, the Lazard Equity Franchise Portfolio rose in absolute terms but underperformed its benchmark, the MSCI World Index. (Excess return is measured net of fees and in US dollars.)

Contributors

  • Shares in Nexi (OTCPK:NEXXY)(6.4% weighting) traded higher after the Italy-based payments company reported solid first-quarter results, highlighted by strong operational performance, thanks to sustained organic growth and improving margins. We believe that the company's ability to effectively extract synergies from past consolidations and its preparedness to navigate the evolving payment landscape will be pivotal in supporting a further share price rerating.
  • Shares of United Utilities (OTCPK:UUGRY)(3.4% weighting) rose after the UK-based water provider reported strong fiscal-year results that highlighted its robust conclusion to the 2020-2025 regulatory period. Shares received an additional boost from United Utilities’s investor day, which bolstered market confidence that the company had efficiently prepared for the step-up in investment for the 2025-2030 period, and that the targeted 100-basis point outperformance was likely conservative. We view the elevated UK inflation rate of 3.4% as a favorable factor for the company. Looking ahead, we believe that improved returns, combined with the scale of the upcoming investment program, will drive significant earnings growth over the next five years.
  • Traders bid up shares of FDJ United (OTCPK:LFDJF)(5.3% weighting) on bullish sentiment about the financial targets disclosed at the France-based gaming giant’s investor day. With a target of 5% organic growth and a margin for earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation, and amortization (EBITDA) exceeding 26% by 2028, the company’s financial plan is significantly above our forecasts, giving us confidence in the potential upside for the stock.

Detractors

  • Shares of US-based advertising and marketing services provider Omnicom (OMC)(6.0% weighting) traded lower after a sell-side downgrade of the agency industry, driven by macroeconomic risks and concerns that artificial intelligence (AI) could disintermediate advertising agencies. While the potential impact of AI remains uncertain, Omnicom has a strong track record of successfully navigating technological changes in the advertising industry. Over the past 15 years, the company has grown free cash flow per share at an annual rate of 8%, during a period of significant technological disruption. This era saw the rise of the internet, search engines, mobile technology, and social media, which transformed the media and advertising landscape, with digital advertising increasing from 20% to 65% of total ad spending. Historically, clients have tended to reinvest savings generated by technology into additional advertising due to competitive pressures, relying on Omnicom’s expertise to manage the added complexity of fragmented media channels. Omnicom is currently trading at an earnings multiple of 8.3, its lowest stock market valuation in 30 years. Our analysis indicates that the current share price implies a ~30% decline in earnings over the next decade—a scenario we believe is not supported by the available evidence.
  • Despite reporting solid first-quarter results that included 7% organic growth, 200 bps of margin expansion and 14% growth in earning per share, Fiserv (FI)(5.3% weighting), a US-based technology provider of payments and financial services, saw its share price fall on concerns slowing quarter-over-quarter growth in gross payment volumes at Clover, the company’s fast growing point of sale and software product for small and medium-sized businesses (SMBs). Nonetheless, Clover revenues grew 27%, and the company has not changed its medium- or long-run revenue targets for this segment. Our valuation for the business has not materially changed, and we believe the share price fall represents an attractive entry point for long-term investors.
  • Traders sold off shares of Baxter International (BAX)(3.8% weighting) on concerns that the US-based medical device maker will be adversely impacted by US tariffs. As a manufacturer in the US and offshore, the tariffs could in theory impact profit margins. However, Baxter has located its manufacturing facilities in the same countries where it sells its products, potentially minimizing the potential impact from goods subject to US import taxes. The company’s guidance for fiscal year 2025 suggested modest impact from the proposed tariffs.

Outlook

The Equity Franchise Portfolio currently holds market leaders and monopolies with higher long-run earnings predictability than those of the market and that are trading at reasonable valuations. The Portfolio is now trading at a sizable discount to intrinsic value, as well as the broader MSCI World Index on a number of valuation measures. We believe the economic franchise characteristics we seek for all our investments will continue to serve our investors well over the long run.

data.title: Franklin Templeton ClearBridge Select Fund Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Key Takeaways

  • Markets: U.S. equities rebounded strongly from tariff and geopolitical scares to deliver healthy gains in the second quarter. The S&P 500 ((SP500), (SPX)) Index returned 10.9%, swinging from a near bear market to an all-time high, while the benchmark Russell 3000 Index advanced 11.0% as risk-on sentiment took hold after tariff implementation was delayed. Growth stocks led across market caps, with the Russell 3000 Growth Index (+17.6%) outperforming its value counterpart by more than 1,300 basis points. The Russell 2500 Growth Index, home to many of the small and mid cap companies we target in the Fund, rose 11.3%.
  • Contributors: Holdings in MercadoLibre (MELI), Vertiv (VRT), Comfort Systems (FIX) and Reddit (RDDT). Stock selection in health care and financials, overweight to information technology (IT).
  • Detractors: Holdings in Copart (CPRT), Baker Hughes (BKR), underweight to Microsoft. Stock selection in IT and industrials.
  • Outlook: We believe more clarity on tariffs will open the logjam of capital, both in capital markets and that held by companies for future investment. A reopening of the IPO market is a positive sign, and we are looking at a number of new ideas among companies planning to go public.

Performance Review

  • The Fund outperformed the benchmark for the period, drawing strength from the balance we have created through our pyramid portfolio construction across companies and sectors with distinct growth drivers.
  • Our health care exposure was a particular standout. Insulet (PODD), a developer of insulin pumps for the treatment of diabetes, is benefiting from Omnipod sales among the type 2 diabetes population, a market it received FDA approval for last summer. HealthEquity (HQY), a provider of health savings accounts (HSA), saw its shares rise on better than expected quarterly revenue and raised full-year guidance driven by strong growth in HSAs and assets. The Fund also saw strong performance from Caris Life Sciences (CAI), an Al-driven company in cancer care and genetic profiling.
  • Vertiv and Comfort Systems continue to benefit from the growing focus on AI and the underlying infrastructure investments required to support it. Vertiv delivers power and thermal management systems critical for data center operations, while Comfort Systems provides HVAC and electrical systems installation and maintenance.
  • These names helped offset weakness across our more cyclical industrial positions. Detractors included auto salvage company Copart, which fell sharply after its latest quarterly revenue missed investor expectations for the first time in many quarters. Willscot (WSC)and Trex (TREX), holdings tied to commercial and residential construction, also underperformed.
  • We initiated seven new positions while exiting four others. Our largest purchase was Verisk Analytics (VRSK), a data and software solutions provider to the insurance industry whose modern solutions are powerful and sticky. We exited Monolithic Power Systems (MPWR), rolling the proceeds into newer semiconductor additions, Lattice Semiconductor (LSCC) and Qorvo (QRVO).
  • We also added Sartorius (OTCPK:SARTF), a premier manufacturer of consumable products and services used in the development of biopharmaceutical production, and Penumbra (PEN), which develops aspirators to remove clots from the brain, heart, lungs and peripheral circulation.

Outlook

  • The market's rally off the early April lows on improving trade sentiment validates our view that the solution to the global tariff problem rests in the hands of the President, whose stance can quickly be reversed. Similarly, much lower rates are not needed for a broadening of performance in equity markets; rates just need to gradually move to a normal level with lower levels of volatility, and business activity needs more clarity on tariffs. Large cap stocks have continued to outperform smaller caps, with the rolling 10-year annualized return gap the largest on record dating back to the 1930s, and relative valuations in the bottom decile. SMID stocks have historically recovered following such periods of sizeable underperformance.
  • Economic data points like a tariff-induced contraction in first-quarter GDP, range-bound inflation and job growth without wage spikes suggest the Fed is likely to move forward with rate cuts in the second half of the year. What is needed is more clarity on tariffs, which would open the logjam of capital, both in capital markets and that held by companies for future investment. We are starting to see a reopening of the IPO market and are looking at a number of new ideas among companies planning to go public, as well as late-stage high-growth innovators.

data.title: The New Industrial Policy Playbook: Intel First, Who's Next?
data.description:
By Christopher Gannatti, CFA & Samuel Rines

Intel's $8.9 Billion Moment

The Trump administration's equity stake in Intel (INTC) marks a turning point in U.S. industrial policy. Unlike past subsidies, this is ownership: 433.3 million shares purchased at $20.47 each, giving Washington a 9.9% stake in America's only leading-edge chipmaker. Importantly, this wasn't fresh cash so much as a conversion of $5.7 billion in CHIPS Act grants and $3.2 billion in Secure Enclave funding layered on top of $2.2 billion already delivered. The symbolism is powerful: permanent capital, not clawbacks and conditional grants.1

The deal is deliberately hands-off. Washington gets no board seat and no day-to-day influence, and its warrant for another 5% stake only activates if Intel loses majority control of its foundry.2 It is an ownership signal, not a management takeover. Taxpayers get equity at a discount, Intel gets a balance sheet backstop without new debt, and the entire industry gets a message: the U.S. government is not just subsidizing semiconductors, it is betting on them.

From a shareholder perspective, the structure is designed to balance political symbolism with financial prudence. For Intel, the benefit is equally clear: a reinforced balance sheet, the elimination of clawback provisions that could have constrained capital planning and a level of confidence that simply writing another grant check could never deliver. Symbolically, this marks a break from subsidy-style industrial policy toward explicit equity participation, with the state standing shoulder to shoulder with private shareholders in assuming risk and return.

Intel's Position: Assets and Struggles

Intel still controls some of the most valuable industrial assets in America: fabs3 in Arizona, Ohio, New Mexico and Oregon, many of which are being tooled for the 18A and planned 14A nodes. These facilities anchor more than $100 billion of recent investment.4 Yet, the same fabs that prove Intel's strategic importance also drag on its financials. Capital expenditures are running at $18 billion annually, while the foundry division posted a $5.5 billion loss in the first half of 2025.5 They are crown jewels—but crown jewels that bleed cash.

Financially, Intel has lost $45 billion in free cash flow in just more than three years, eroding a market capitalization now down 64% from its 2020 peak. Gross margins, once reliably 55%–60%, hover in the mid-30s. Execution has faltered too: of the vaunted "five nodes6 in four years" pledge, only Intel 4 counts as a clear success. External foundry revenue in 2025 was just $53 million—symbolic in the face of multibillion-dollar losses. The exit of Pat Gelsinger, replaced by Lip-Bu Tan, underlined how fragile confidence had become. As Barron's observed, "money alone won't solve the company's problems, which are rooted in manufacturing."7

Analysts highlight the central bind: Intel needs both scale and credibility to fund its roadmap, but its repeated missteps have eroded confidence. Without a stronger central processing unit (CPU) roadmap and market-share recovery, Intel lacks the scale to justify the very investments it is making.8 That, more than any headline, explains why the government stepped in. It wasn't saving a company that was "just fine"; it was preventing a national champion from failing through sheer lack of time and customers.

Ways to Win: Why the Stake Matters9

The Intel stake works because it multiplies paths to success. The first is simple downside protection: permanent capital that reduces the chance of outright failure. With Washington on the cap table,10 the probability of insolvency collapses.

The second path is confidence-driven. If the government refuses to let Intel fail, then hyperscalers,11 defense primes and fabless12 U.S. chipmakers can rationalize new wafer orders. Notably, the government explicitly hates failures. One anchor contract at 14A13 could spark a hedging cascade; others would follow to preserve optionality. The dynamic here is less about present competitiveness than about reducing counterparty risk. When Washington backs a company, customers feel safer making long-dated bets.

The third path is policy leverage. Intel becomes a bargaining chip in broader negotiations: access to U.S. markets, favorable tariffs or accelerated expensing might tilt toward customers who support onshore Intel production. These fiscal levers matter more than subsidies because they change the total cost of ownership. Washington does not have to tell customers, "Buy Intel." It simply has to make it financially smarter to do so.

Finally, there's the breakup option. Intel has already carved out its Foundry unit. The government's warrant is explicitly tied to its control. A clean separation with Foundry as a national champion, and CPUs and datacenter assets free to find their own partners, could unlock value that the integrated model cannot. History offers precedent: Standard Oil's breakup revealed more value in the parts than the whole. Each path, be it downside protection, customer orders, fiscal favoritism or breakup, creates more ways to win than to lose.

What Comes after Intel?

The bigger lesson is that the Intel stake is not an anomaly. It signals a new playbook: whenever a company sits at a chokepoint, lacks the financial scale to compete and matters for both security and economics, do nothing is not the base case. Washington now has equity, procurement and tariff levers it is willing to use. That changes the distribution of likely outcomes across the industry.

Continuing this thread, in an August 26, 2025, interview on CNBC, Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick revealed that the administration is "actively discussing" stakes in major defense and tech contractors, including Lockheed Martin, Boeing and Palantir.14

Other potential candidates stand out. Each has reasons for and against intervention. Our aim here is not to predict or call out the next action of the U.S. government, but rather to present an illustrative mental model that presents a way of trying to think of the sorts of things the U.S. government may think of in the future:15

  • GlobalFoundries (GF) (GFS)

Why it might happen: GF is critical for autos, defense and radio frequency chips, ensuring resilience in supply chains most exposed to shocks. It also represents a U.S.-domiciled alternative to Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Co. (TSMC) (TSM) for less-advanced but strategically vital nodes.

Why it might not: The company already has stable niches and profitability at the lagging edge;16 the government may view it as less urgent since it does not compete directly in the bleeding edge where national security concerns are most acute.

  • Micron (MU)

Why it might happen: Micron anchors U.S. memory and high-bandwidth memory (HBM) for AI accelerators.17 HBM, in particular, is a national security interest, given its role in AI compute.

Why it might not: Micron is relatively healthy financially compared to Intel, and while capital hungry, it has demonstrated execution strength. Interventions would likely be more targeted (loan guarantees or procurement) rather than equity.

  • Wolfspeed (WOLF)

Why it might happen: Wolfspeed is central to silicon carbide (SiC) semiconductors, which underpin electric vehicle (EV) drivetrains, power electronics and defense systems. Its balance sheet has been stretched by fab delays, making it a candidate for risk-sharing capital.

Why it might not: The SiC market, while strategic, is smaller in scale than logic or memory.18 Washington might conclude that demand pull-through, rather than ownership, is enough to support Wolfspeed.

  • Amkor/Advanced Packaging (AMKR)

Why it might happen: Advanced packaging is the hidden chokepoint of the AI era, and U.S. capacity is limited. A national packaging utility could be a natural candidate for public–private capital.

Why it might not: Politically, "packaging" lacks the resonance of "chips," and subsidies may flow more naturally than equity. A shared facility funded through procurement may be viewed as sufficient.

  • Coherent (Lasers/Optics) (COHR)

Why it might happen: Coherent plays a role in lasers, optics and photonics critical for interconnect and defense. Fragile financial performance could create pressure for support.

Why it might not: Compared to memory or foundry, photonics is a layer removed from the AI/logic core. Washington may prioritize more direct chokepoints before turning to optics.

Each is imperfect, but that is the point: Intel, too, was imperfect when Washington acted. The calculus is not about the U.S. government backing winners, but about reducing the chance of strategically vital failures. Investors should see this as an emerging policy pattern: chokepoint companies with weak balance sheets now have more ways to win than the market currently prices.

Closing Thought

Washington's stake in Intel is not about "picking winners" in the caricatured sense. It is about reducing failure risk in industries too strategic to leave exposed. Equity, not subsidy, may be the new currency of industrial policy. The question is no longer whether government will step in, but where and how—and which chokepoints it chooses to reinforce. Intel shows that the era of subsidies-only industrial policy is over. What comes next may be smaller, subtler and industry-specific, but the direction is clear: if the U.S. government sees a company as indispensable, "just fine on its own" may never be the starting assumption again.

For those considering how WisdomTree places Intel into either the geopolitical or artificial intelligence contexts:

  • WisdomTree GeoAlpha Opportunities Fund (GEOA): GEOA is designed to track the total return performance of, before fees and expenses, the WisdomTree GeoAlpha Opportunities Index. This strategy generates exposure to companies primarily benefiting from geopolitical and global policy shifts. It focuses on the following categories of geopolitical and global policy shifts: geopolitical events, fiscal and monetary policy shifts, innovations in technology and shifting consumer preferences. As of August 22, 2025, Intel was a 2.78% weight in GEOA. Holdings are subject to change.
  • WisdomTree Artificial Intelligence & Innovation Fund (WTAI): WTAI is designed to track the total return performance of, before fees and expenses, the WisdomTree Artificial Intelligence & Innovation Index. As of August 22, 2025, Intel was a 1.55% weight in WTAI. Holdings are subject to change.

Footnotes

1 Source: Intel Corporation, "Intel and Trump Administration reach historic agreement to accelerate American technology and manufacturing leadership" [press release], Business Wire, 8/22/25.

2 Source: Intel Corporation, 2025.

3 Fabs refers to facilities that directly make physical semiconductors.

4 Source: Intel Corporation, 2025.

5 Source: Intel Corporation. "Form 10-Q for the quarterly period ended June 29, 2025," U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission, 2025.

6 Nodes refer to "process nodes" that are usually defined in relation to the brand label for a generation of process technology—capturing improvements in performance, power efficiency and density, but not tied to a literal nanometer metric.

7 Source: Barron's, "Intel's $10 billion lifeline: Why Washington's stake may not be enough," Dow Jones & Company, 8/21/25.

8 Source: J. Moore, M. Wayne, S. Brett, E. Tulchinsky and N. Kozhukhov, "Intel Corporation (INTC): What's next for Intel?", Morgan Stanley Research, 8/21/25.

9 Sources for the "Ways to Win" section: Intel Corporation, "Intel and Trump Administration reach historic agreement to accelerate American technology and manufacturing leadership" [press release], Business Wire, 8/22/25; Intel Corporation, "Form 10-Q for the quarterly period ended June 29, 2025," U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission, 2025; J. Moore, M. Wayne, S. Brett, E. Tulchinsky and N. Kozhukhov, "Intel Corporation (INTC): What's next for Intel?", Morgan Stanley Research, 8/21/25.

10 The cap table references the list of owners, usually equity stakes, of a company.

11 Hyperscalers refers to the largest providers of public cloud infrastructure, typically Amazon Web Services, Microsoft Azure, Google Cloud and, more recently, Oracle.

12 A fabless chipmaker would design chips and send those designs to a fabrication facility.

13 14A refers to a process node that Intel is currently working on and would represent a significant technological leap forward.

14 Source: "U.S. could seek piece of defense companies, Commerce chief says" [news article], Reuters, 8/26/25.

15 Sources for following bullet points: each specified firm's 10-K and 10-Q filings.

16 Lagging edge refers to less advanced chips that may be important and in demand but that do not require the most advanced technologies to make.

17 HBM chips are essential to be used alongside AI accelerator graphics processing units, such as Nvidia's Blackwell GPU.

18 Logic refers to chips that process information, like CPUs and GPUs. Memory refers to chips that store the results of computations done by logic chips.

Important Risks Related to this Article

For current holdings, please click the respective ticker: GEOA, WTAI. Holdings are subject to risk and change.

There are risks associated with investing, including the possible loss of principal. Please read the Fund’s prospectus for specific details regarding the Fund’s risk profile.

Companies engaged in AI typically face intense competition and potentially rapid product obsolescence. These companies are also heavily dependent on intellectual property rights and may be adversely affected by loss or impairment of those rights. Additionally, AI companies typically invest significant amounts of spending on research and development, and there is no guarantee that the products or services produced by these companies will be successful. Companies that are capitalizing on innovation and developing technologies to displace older technologies or create new markets may not be successful. The Fund invests in the securities included in, or representative of, its Index regardless of their investment merit, and the Fund does not attempt to outperform its Index or take defensive positions in declining markets. The composition of the Index is governed by an Index Committee, and the Index may not perform as intended.

GEOA: Some countries and regions in which the Fund invests may have and may continue to experience security concerns, war, aggression and/or conflict, economic uncertainty, sanctions or the threat of sanctions, natural and environmental disasters, and widespread disease or other public health issues. Foreign investing involves special risks, such as risk of loss from currency fluctuation or political or economic uncertainty. Investments in non-U.S. securities involve political, regulatory and economic risks that may not be present in investments in U.S. securities. To the extent the Fund invests a significant portion of its assets in a single country or region, it is more likely to be impacted by events affecting that country or region.

Christopher Gannatti, CFA, Global Head of Research

Christopher Gannatti began at WisdomTree as a Research Analyst in December 2010, working directly with Jeremy Schwartz, CFA®, Director of Research. In January of 2014, he was promoted to Associate Director of Research where he was responsible to lead different groups of analysts and strategists within the broader Research team at WisdomTree. In February of 2018, Christopher was promoted to Head of Research, Europe, where he was based out of WisdomTree’s London office and was responsible for the full WisdomTree research effort within the European market, as well as supporting the UCITs platform globally. In November 2021, Christopher was promoted to Global Head of Research, now responsible for numerous communications on investment strategy globally, particularly in the thematic equity space. Christopher came to WisdomTree from Lord Abbett, where he worked for four and a half years as a Regional Consultant. He received his MBA in Quantitative Finance, Accounting, and Economics from NYU’s Stern School of Business in 2010, and he received his bachelor’s degree from Colgate University in Economics in 2006. Christopher is a holder of the Chartered Financial Analyst Designation.

Samuel Rines, Macro Strategist, Model Portfolios

Sam Rines serves as a Macro Strategist, Model Portfolios at WisdomTree extending the firm’s custom model portfolio management capabilities. Samuel is a global macro expert focused on the investment implications of politics and policy. His PolyMacro research has been widely followed by large family offices, institutional investors and the media. Prior to joining CORBU, Samuel was the Chief Economist and Investment Strategist at Avalon Advisors. Before joining Avalon, Samuel was a portfolio manager at Chilton Capital Management, where he launched the Chilton ESG Equity Strategy and a long/short technology portfolio. Samuel started his career as the cross-asset analyst for a small hedge fund. He is the author of the book “After Normal: Making Sense of the Global Economy ”.

data.title: EQT: The Smart Money Is Bullish, Buy The Dip
data.description:
I last covered Appalachia-focused natural gas producer EQT Corp. (NYSE:EQT) back on February 21, 2025 in a piece titled “EQT: Raising Target on Strong Free Cash Flow Upside.”

In it I reiterated my strong buy recommendation covered in a piece in May 2024 titled “EQT: Cementing Position as Low-Cost Gas Producer,” and raised my target on the stock from $56 to $70 per share.

Since my piece just over six months ago, EQT shares are up a little over 2.8%, outperforming the popular SPDR S&P Oil & Gas Exploration and Production ETF (XOP) by 4.6 percentage points:

However, while EQT is up and has outperformed the industry benchmark XOP fund, it’s down about 13 percent from its late June 2025 peak over $60 per share.

There’s little doubt what’s catalyzed the selling pressure this summer:

This chart shows a close-up of the trading action in two natural gas producers – EQT and Expand Energy (EXE) – since May 1st plotted alongside the spot price of Henry Hub natural gas over the same time frame.

As you can see, gas prices dipped into early June, then rallied again into July. However, since roughly mid-July, gas prices have retreated, dragging down both EQT and EXE.

As I'll explain in this article, while the spot and front-month gas futures contracts have been hit since late June, futures for delivery into early 2026 are trading at healthy prices at or over $4/MMBtu. Moreover, the smart money -- gas producers and other hedgers -- are reducing their price hedges into early 2026, a sign they believe the dip in gas is temporary.

Bottom line: The selloff in EQT is overdone, and that represents an outstanding buying opportunity in the stock right now.

Natural Gas Prices

A deep dive into the myriad drivers of natural gas prices this summer is a bit beyond the scope of this update.

Instead, let’s keep it simple.

In the summer months, the key to US natural gas demand is electricity generation. For example, in July of last year (July 2024) the Energy Information Administration (EIA) estimated total US consumption of natural gas was 88.8 bcf/day (this doesn’t include exports).

Of that 88.8 bcf/day, some 49.5 bcf/day (about 55.7%) was used to produce electric power, while 22.1 bcf/day (24.9%) was used in the industrial sector, a category that includes gas used in oil refining, chemical production, and manufacturing.

That should come as little surprise since electricity demand rises in the summer to power air conditioning systems, and natural gas is the most important fuel for electricity generation in the US. Per EIA, natural gas accounted for 48.3% of net electricity generation in July 2024, followed by coal at 16.7% and nuclear at 16.2%.

With that in mind, look at this chart:

The EIA’s Short-Term Energy Outlook (STEO) contains energy-related data on a historical basis as well as projections through next year.

This chart shows average US Cooling Degree Days (CDDs) for the months of May through September -- the heart of cooling season -- since 2014. The chart includes historical data through July 2025, and EIA projections out to September 2026.

CDDs measure average temperatures in the US by month compared to a baseline level of 65 degrees Fahrenheit (roughly 18 Celsius). The higher the CDDs, the hotter the weather, and higher the electricity demand for cooling.

I’ve drawn an arrow to indicate this summer on my chart. Specifically, the arrow points to July 2025, and July is, in most years, a peak month for cooling degree days. As you can see, CDDs this summer have been below the levels we saw in the summers of 2022, 2023, and 2024.

On this basis, 2025 is shaping up to be the coolest summer since 2021 for the US.

Indeed, EIA estimates demand for natural gas from the electric power sector fell 1.7 bcf/day in July of this year compared to July 2024; in August 2025 EIA estimates demand will be down 1.5 bcf/day from the same month one year ago.

US gas in storage is now back above the 5-year seasonal average:

This chart comes from the EIA’s Weekly Natural Gas Storage Report.

The orange line shows the total amount of natural gas storage EIA reports each week since the first week of 2023 while the blue line shows the 5-year seasonal average for each week of the year.

The columns at the bottom of my chart show the deviation between the latest data and the 5-year seasonal average. I’ve color-coded the columns such that red bars show a surplus of storage to the 5-year seasonal average (bearish gas prices) while a green column signifies a deficit of gas storage on the same basis.

So, you can clearly see US natural gas storage was below the seasonal norm in the spring of this year; yet, has since risen to as high as 196 billion cubic feet (bcf) above average this summer amid weak natural gas power burn. As of the latest release on Thursday, August 28th, US gas storage is sitting a little over 150 bcf above the 5-year average.

However, two points are worth noting.

First, the scale of excess gas in storage this summer pales in comparison to what we saw in the summers of 2023 and 2024 when the surfeit of gas was as much as 678 bcf above the 5-year seasonal average.

Second, the “smart money” in the futures market doesn’t appear to believe prices will remain weak.

Let me explain:

Hedgers aren’t Selling Gas

Every week, the Commodity Futures Trading Commission (CFTC) releases its Commitment of Traders (COT) Report, which details futures and options market positions on the NYMEX-traded natural gas futures market.

As for all commodities, CFTC divides traders into two broad categories – Commercials and Non-Commercials.

Simply put, commercials represent companies using the futures and options market to hedge business risks. For example, a gas producer like EQT might sell gas futures or buy put options on gas to protect cash flows from falling commodity prices.

Alternatively, an oil refiner might buy futures to guard against a spike in operating costs due to a rise in natural gas prices.

Non-Commercials are speculators -- traders and investors who have no business exposure to gas but are simply seeking to profit from price fluctuations.

The commercials are sometimes dubbed the “smart money,” because these companies would be expected to have greater insight into industry supply and demand conditions that speculators (non-commercials in the COT report). For example, a producer like EQT or EXE likely has insight into drilling and production activity in key gas-producing fields like the Marcellus Shale in Appalachia and the Haynesville Shale of Louisiana and uses this information to inform their hedging decisions.

Here's a chart of the net position – gross longs less gross shorts -- held by commercials in the NYMEX natural gas market over the past few years:

Simply put, a rising line on this chart tells us one of two things.

First, hedgers could be boosting long positions; for example, a gas consumer worried about higher gas prices might buy futures or options to hedge against a price spike.

Alternatively, it could mean that gas producers are hedging less. They’re not shorting as much gas or buying puts to protect against a decline in prices because they expect the price of gas to be higher in future months than is currently baked into the futures curve.

I like to use this data in two ways. First, I like to look for extremes - large short or long positions in gas futures that could indicate hedgers are looking for a major rise or fall in natural gas prices.

Second, I like to watch commercial activity as it relates to the price of natural gas:

This chart shows the weekly price of natural gas in the US since late 2021 from EIA, the same period covered by my chart of net commercial gas positions just above.

I’ve labeled some of the same dates on this chart as I labeled on the commercials chart.

For example, note that in October 2023 gas prices were on the rise above $3.20/MMBtu and that the net commercial long position spiked down to under 35,000 contracts, the lowest level of any week since late 2021.

This suggests that commercials were bearish gas or, at a minimum, felt that prices around $3.20 represented a high price for the commodity given underlying fundamentals.

Let me add in a layer of granularity based on the raw data in COT. The absolute low for the commercial net position in October 2023 was +32,543. In the five weeks leading up to that date, the gross long position fell a little under 20,000 contracts while the gross short position soared 38,824.

In other words, most of the drop in the commercial net position back in October 2023 was due to a surge in new short positions. That strongly suggests producers used the rally in gas to around $3.20/MMBtu to step up their hedging activity -- selling gas and buying puts -- to lock in those prices for future production.

The commercials were “right” about gas, and prices slid well under $2/MMBTu as the glut of gas in storage worsened in early 2024.

In May and August of last year, we saw similar downward spikes in the net commercial gas position. Once again, these dates also corresponded to a jump in the commercial gross short position for gas:

I’ve labeled some clear spikes in gross short positions in late May and early August 2024 suggesting a pick-up in hedging activity.

While gas prices were off their spring 2024 lows last summer, spot and front-month futures prices were still trading at depressed levels. However, as I pointed out in my EQT article here on Seeking Alpha last year, gas futures for delivery in Q4 2024 were trading around $3.00/MMBtu, and for Q1 2025 prices were north of $3.50/MMBtu.

Most likely, the spike on commercial gross short positions reflects producers locking in those prices of $3.00+/MMBtu for late 2024 and early this year last summer.

Regardless, gas prices remained weak until late last year, when the combination of cold winter weather and a surge in liquefied natural gas (LNG) exports conspired to drive gas sharply higher. The surge in commercial hedging activity was a good early warning signal of last summer’s price weakness.

And that brings me to the current situation. Specifically, while the front-month price of natural gas right now is under $3.00/MMBtu, the futures curve looks much healthier:

The blue line on this chart shows the closing price for each monthly NYMEX natural gas futures contract from September 2025 (the final day of trading was Wednesday, August 27th) through December 2027 sourced from CME. The red line tracks the quarterly calendar strips over this time period; for example, the Q1 2026 strip would be the average price of the January, February and March 2026 gas futures contracts (about $3.85/MMBtu right now).

Simply put, the front month price of gas futures is below $3/MMBtu right now and has fallen this summer due, in large part, to the weather-related catalysts I outlined earlier.

However, the futures market has baked in a significant increase in gas prices into early 2026. For example, if you wish to contract for gas delivery in January 2026 right now the cost would be $4.08/MMBtu. Moreover, look at the curve further into the future. Starting in 2026, the lowest quarterly calendar strip for gas is $3.49/MMBtu and the average price of gas for delivery from January 2026 through December 2027 is almost $3.90/MMBtu.

And what’s interesting is that despite the attractive futures curve for natural gas, we’re not seeing a big decline in the net commercial position. Indeed, since mid-July, gross commercial shorts have declined by close to 26,000 contracts, suggesting a lack of hedging activity this summer despite prices far higher than they were in the summer of last year.

EQT and Expand Hedging

EQT and Expand are the two largest US shale gas producers, with a total expected 2025 gas production of roughly 6.5 and 7.1 bcf/day respectively.

So rather than just relying on the industry data inferred from the COT report, I also like to watch how these two large, savvy producers hedge their commodity price exposure.

Take a look:

This top half of this table shows EQT’s reported hedge positions as of July 19, 2024 (last summer) included in its Q2 2024 earnings results presentation.

Meanwhile, the bottom half of this chart shows the company's reported hedge position as of July 15, 2025 included as part of their Q2 2025 earnings release.

As you can see, EQT’s hedges for the final two quarters of 2025 are of a similar magnitude to its hedges covering the final two quarters of 2024 one year ago. On its Q2 2024 call, management estimated it had hedged about 60% of its expected Q3 and Q4 2024 production and this year EQT estimates it has hedged around 55% of Q3 and Q4 2025 volumes.

However, the big shift is for the year ahead.

In July 2024, EQT had hedged 772 billion cubic feet of expected 2025 production, heavily weighted to favor hedges through the first half of this year. Moreover, around 70% of those hedges were structured as swaps rather than put options.

Swaps lock in a specific price for future gas production, so when you hedge with swaps you’re “giving away” any potential upside for gas. Put options and options collar strategies provide a producer with a floor price for gas; however if gas does rally, the producer still gets to benefit from some or all of that upside, depending upon the exact strategies employed.

As of mid-July this year, however, EQT had only 166 bcf of hedges for all of 2026 and all those hedges were structured as options strategies.

That’s a clear signal that one year ago, EQT had some concerns about gas prices, through the first half of this year. Today, however, the fact they’re both lightly hedged and have no swaps covering 2026 output, suggests management sees upside for gas above the prices currently factored in to futures curve I just outlined.

The situation for Expand is a bit more complex because one year ago it had not completed its acquisition of Southwestern Energy; the Southwestern deal closed in October.

However, according to the company’s Q2 2024 earnings presentation, Expand (then known as Chesapeake Energy) had hedged 60% of Q3 and Q4 2024 production and a total of 443 bcf of 2025 production as of July 24, 2024. As of Q2 2025 the company – July 22, 2025 to be exact – EXE reported it had hedged 58% of expected Q3 and Q4 2025 output and 985 bcf through next year.

However, while hedges for EXE are higher in absolute terms this year, the company is much larger as well thanks to the Southwestern deal. Total EXE production in 2025 is expected to be around 7.1 bcf/day while in 2023, the last full year before the Southwestern deal, the company produced less than 3.7 bcf/day.

Conclusion and Risks

Natural gas prices are notoriously volatile. Spot and front-month futures contracts often see dramatic moves due to changes in near-term weather trends or updated forecasts.

And severe sell-offs in front-month gas prices can weigh on the stocks of gas producers like EQT Corp, as we’ve seen over the past two months.

However, from a fundamental standpoint, what really matters isn’t the price of gas in August or September 2025, but the average price of gas through the year as well as, of course, a particular producer’s cost structure and ability to generate free cash flow.

The good news is that even with front-month futures trading under $3.00/MMBtu the average price of gas for delivery between January 2026 and December 2027 is neat $3.90/MMBtu.

Moreover, the lack of producer hedging apparent in the CFTC's Commitment of Traders Report as well as in EQT's own results, suggests the smart money sees current weakness in gas as temporary. EQT's light hedging through 2026, in particular, appears to reflect a view that current futures market pricing for 2026 is too low.

Temporary weakness in gas prices this summer due to short-term weather-related concerns has opened a compelling buying opportunity in EQT.

The biggest risk for EQT is a significant, prolonged decline in gas prices into early 2026. In such a scenario, EQT's free cash flow would be impacted both by the decline in price and the lack of protective hedges.

However, according to management's own estimates, a calculation backed up by the free cash flow models I included in my piece on EQT earlier this year, the company can still generate more than $2 billion in annual free cash flow even with gas at $2.75/MMBTu, well below the current quote.

In short, even in a bearish commodity scenario, EQT's low production costs give it a competitive advantage over peers.

EQT continues to rate a strong buy.

data.title: Franklin LifeSmart Funds Q2 2025 Review
data.description:
The LifeSmart Portfolios delivered healthy performance during the second quarter, particularly longer-dated vintages, demonstrating resilience during a period marked by volatility and a significant market drawdown at the start.

Regional allocations in equity supported relative performance, as did underweight allocations to fixed income

How were market conditions in the second quarter of calendar 2025?

Global equity markets collectively rose during the second quarter after bouncing back from their April lows. The quarter began with US President Donald Trump's announcement of reciprocal tariffs that were more sweeping and severe than many expected, leading to significant financial market volatility and equity market declines. However, global trade tensions eased during the rest of the quarter as the United States delayed planned tariff hikes, reducing investor fears of a global recession. The onset of the Israel-Iran conflict in the Middle East in mid-June and its tenuous ceasefire at month-end had minimal impact on global equity markets.

In the United States, stocks advanced during the second quarter of 2025. After rebounding from April's lows, equity markets continued to rally through June despite bouts of volatility. After dropping significantly in early April, the S&P 500 ((SP500), (SPX)) Index trended higher and closed the period with solid gains after hitting new record highs at the end of June, marking a sharp turnaround from the start of the quarter. The technology-heavy Nasdaq (NDAQ) Composite Index performed strongly during the period, bolstered by better-than-expected earnings reports from certain mega-capitalization, tech-related companies; despite experiencing heightened volatility, it hit new record highs in late June and had its best quarter since 2020.

The US Federal Reserve (Fed) maintained its stance during the quarter. It held the federal funds target rate steady in May and June at a range of 4.25%–4.50% as policymakers continued to monitor and evaluate the potential impact of tariff, immigration and tax policies. A revised projection in June indicated that the Fed continued to see two 25-basis point (BP) rate cuts by the end of the year. Annual inflation, as measured by the core personal consumption expenditures price index—which excludes food and energy prices and is the Fed's preferred gauge—slowed in April for the second consecutive month but accelerated in May, remaining above the Fed's 2% long-run target.

European stocks collectively gained in the second quarter, driven by a shift in investor sentiment. Some easing in trade tensions and geopolitical risks (especially after the Israel-Iran ceasefire) supported European equity markets. Additionally, progress in trade negotiations between the United States and the European Union boosted confidence and helped ease fears of a global recession, as did the 90-day truce on US-China reciprocal and retaliatory tariffs in May. The European Central Bank cut its key interest rates by 25 basis points (BPs) in April and another 25 bps in June, largely due to updated inflation and economic forecasts.

Over the period, US Treasury (UST) yield changes were mixed. Very short- and intermediate-dated yields witnessed declines, while longer-maturity UST yields increased.

How did the glide path—or starting point for each portfolio—impact performance during the three months ended June 30, 2025?

LifeSmart Retirement Fund portfolios posted healthy gains on an absolute performance basis but trailed their underlying benchmarks for the second quarter. On the one hand, relative returns were hampered by a slight overweight allocation to equity to start the quarter, when global stock markets experienced a drawdown. We then lowered equity exposure in April as we assessed the impact of ongoing tariff policy and amid heightened economic uncertainty. A small cash allocation also detracted.

However, we subsequently added equity exposure as the quarter progressed and ended the period relatively even weight, boosting upside participation during the stock market rally. In addition, the portfolios were generally underweight fixed income during the period, which strengthened relative returns. Returns for Advisor Class ranged from 3.35% to 13.97%.

Each LifeSmart Retirement Fund invests in a mix of stocks and bonds, creating a diversified target-date portfolio designed for a retirement saver expecting to retire around the date in each trust's name. We use an important tool called a glide path to adjust allocations among asset classes over time, helping reduce risk approaching the target date. With the glide path as a reference point, our portfolio managers make tactical decisions and invest in actively managed strategies, seeking to outperform their indexbased benchmarks.

What strategies contributed to or detracted from benchmark-relative performance?

Performance was mixed in terms of strategies and regional allocations. Starting with equity, the portfolios were overweight the United States and underweight international and (in applicable portfolios) emerging markets, and this positioning supported relative results.

On an underlying fund level, Franklin Growth Fund was a leading contributor, with Franklin U.S. Large Cap Multifactor Index ETF and Templeton Developing Markets Fund also boosting relative performance.

Conversely, overall fund selection in US and international equity weighed on returns. In fixed income, an overweight allocation to the United States, including exposure to USTs, detracted. An offbenchmark allocation to alternatives also dampened results.

Looking at underlying funds, Putnam Large Cap Value Fund and Franklin International Core Equity Fund weakened relative returns, although the latter fund still delivered double-digit gains for the period and Putnam Large Cap Value Fund outperformed it style benchmark index. In alternatives, Franklin Systematic Style Premia ETF detracted.

In shorter-vintage, fixed-income-oriented LifeSmart funds, Franklin High Yield Corporate ETF and Franklin Investment Grade Corporate ETF both strengthened relative performance as spreads tightened during the second quarter. In contrast, Franklin U.S Core Bond ETF weighed on returns, in part due to UST exposure.

In total, how did the glide path, tactical allocation, and security selection impact performance relative to peers?

In near-retirement vintages, we increase allocations to more defensive assets and integrate flexibility to retreat from equity during turbulent markets, especially during the retirement savings home stretch.

What is your near-term outlook for the markets?

As we enter July, we have seen a strong rally in global equities since tariff-induced volatility in early April. Stocks have consistently navigated pockets of volatility to move higher, fueled by resilient macro data and positive news flow around trade negotiations. But, as equity markets climb back toward highs last seen in February, it leaves us asking whether valuations and sentiment have once again become too stretched.

Leading indicators of economic growth appear to have strengthened during June, suggesting that the global economy may avoid a recession. However, significant trade and geopolitical uncertainty remains, and has yet to be fully reflected in asset prices, in our view.

Macroeconomic data, including indicators of inflation, growth and employment, remain relatively robust in the United States. Even soft data, which weakened sharply amid the onset of trade uncertainty, has improved in the past month. Leading growth indicators have strengthened, while confidence among consumers and corporate chief executive officers has stabilized following the shock of Liberation Day tariffs that have largely been rolled back. In our view, this upturn in data supports the market's rally thus far but is offset by current valuations.

Looking ahead, equity markets are likely to become choppier in the near term, in our view, as factors such as energy price volatility and tariff deadlines increase the possibility of corrections. However, a recession-led drawdown appears more remote to us than it did last month. As a result, we retain our neutral positioning in equities at a cross-asset level.

Looking at international markets, European equities have been a beneficiary of investment outflows from the United States and the likelihood of expanded fiscal spending linked to defense. A dovish European Central Bank is likely to support the eurozone economy, but its export-oriented nature leaves it vulnerable to US tariffs, which may yet be imposed above the baseline 10%. In addition, the temporary impulse from front running tariffs has started to wane and is reflected in slowing industrial production and sharp earnings downgrades. Taking this into account, we remain neutral as we await developments around trade.

In contrast, we hold a cautious view of fixed income, as monetary policy expectations across many economies have become more dovish and have reduced the attractiveness of current yields. US fiscal deficit concerns also influence our decision, along with tight credit spreads, which do not appear to fully reflect economic vulnerability.

data.title: German Unemployment At Highest Level In More Than 10 Years
data.description:
By Carsten Brzeski

In Germany, three million is not just a number. In the context of the labour market, it represents a symbolic threshold - one that separates strength from weakness. Although this benchmark is entirely arbitrary, today’s labour market data will probably leave a larger mark on the public debate than the trend of the last few years. According to the just-released data, German unemployment rose by 45,700, bringing the unemployment figure to 3.025 million, the highest level since early 2015. Seasonally adjusted unemployment dropped by 9,000, keeping the unemployment rate unchanged at 6.3%.

Despite the symbolic weight of the three-million mark, the deterioration of the German labour market is not a sudden development but part of a longer trend. Since reaching a low of 2.2 million in May 2022, the number of unemployed has steadily increased. This trajectory reflects textbook economics: with the economy effectively stagnating for over five years and industry facing severe structural challenges, a worsening of the labour market was just a matter of time.

Things will get worse before they get better

Looking ahead, the tentative signs of a bottoming out of the labour market have given way to a more pessimistic outlook. At least in the short run. After some improvements in the spring, recruitment plans in both industry and services have moved sideways, and the number of vacancies is still dropping. However, a few indicators, like the labour agency’s own sentiment indicator and social media hiring indicators, do at least point to some bottoming out. At the same time, ongoing announcements of potential cost-cutting measures in the automotive and other industries, and the continuing increase in the number of bankruptcies, suggest that things could get worse before they get better.

To be clear, the demographic impact on the labour market and ongoing labour shortages should prevent a sharp worsening, and the market remains far from revisiting the symbolic - and once alarming - milestone of five million unemployed, a figure that triggered the economic reform agenda of the early 2000s. Still, even if the increase in the number of unemployed remains gradual, the risk of an underlying loss in disposable income and broader economic prosperity is high.

data.title: Franklin International Growth Fund Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Key Takeaways

  • Markets: After heightened volatility at the start of the second quarter of 2025 following the announcement of sweeping US tariffs, global equities rebounded strongly to end higher for the period overall, with investor sentiment buoyed by easing trade tensions and resilient economic data.
  • Contributors: Stock selection in the information technology (IT) and consumer discretionary sectors bolstered returns relative to the MSCI (MSCI) EAFE Index-NR.
  • Detractors: Select holdings in the health care sector hindered relative returns.
  • Outlook: Looking ahead, US trade policy seems likely to drive further volatility in the coming quarter, particularly given the expected expiry of the "reciprocal" tariff postponement.

Performance Review

  • In the IT sector, Japan-based chip equipment manufacturer Disco (OTCPK:DSCSY) benefited from investor optimism about the positive impact of rising artificial intelligence (AI) demand on the chip-tool market, as well as the lowering of trade tensions between the United States and China. The company's fiscal fourth-quarter 2024 earnings (released in April) surpassed consensus estimates and its guidance showed an estimated increase in shipments.
  • In the consumer discretionary sector, Latin American e-commerce and fintech firm MercadoLibre (MELI) posted strong first-quarter 2025 results, comfortably exceeding consensus revenue and earnings expectations. The company's operations in Argentina drove the strong performance, helped by improving economic conditions in the country, while its fintech operations also showed impressive growth, especially in credit cards.
  • In contrast, among health care companies, Alcon (ALC), a Swiss-American eye care product provider, reported first-quarter results that came in below consensus estimates, due to weaker trends in implantable intraocular lenses, contact lenses and surgical equipment, notably in the US market. We consider concerns around these trends to be overdone, since the company's new product cycles launching later in the year are likely to accelerate growth, in our view.

Outlook

  • Looking ahead, US trade policy seems likely to drive further volatility in the coming quarter, particularly given the expected expiry of the "reciprocal" tariff postponement. Our view here is unchanged, in that we expect events to remain fluid and uncertain until the back-and-forth between trading partners becomes more settled. Tariff-related concerns about slowing economic growth or recession, higher inflation and supply-chain disruptions appear to have diminished for the time being. However, we remain mindful that economic data is backward-looking, and while inventory management has afforded many companies the capacity to mitigate tariff impacts, that flexibility is likely to be only temporary.
  • As we look to capture a diversified range of growth drivers and economic exposures throughout the global economy, which we believe helps mitigate risk over the long term, we continue to stress-test the business models of the companies in which we have invested for different scenarios, ensuring financial viability and assessing longer-term implications for growth and competitive positioning. Our focus remains on identifying companies that, in our analysis, have strong fundamentals, limited balance sheet risk and robust pricing power, which we believe should help them weather potential periods of turmoil or economic weakness.

data.title: India's GST Reset: Driving Into Tax Cuts
data.description:
By Christopher Gannatti, CFA & Samuel Rines

The Policy Shock That Could Rewire Consumption

India is on the cusp of a policy move that could redefine its consumption cycle. Prime Minister Narendra Modi, in his August 15 Independence Day address, announced "next generation GST reforms" to be implemented before Diwali 2025.1 The Finance Ministry has since outlined a plan for a two-tier GST system—5% for essentials and 18% for standard goods.2

The change is more than just administrative simplification. It has teeth: Small cars and many SUVs, currently taxed effectively at 29%–31% or higher, may fall to 18%. That is a direct reduction in household outlay on India's most aspirational consumer good—the passenger vehicle.

It's possible that this could deliver a 0.5%–0.6% of gross domestic product (GDP) equivalent fiscal stimulus, trimming consumer price index (CPI) inflation by ~40 basis points (bps) and lifting GDP growth by 50–70 bps.3 These are macro numbers with corporate balance sheet implications. In particular, they might set up India's two most important automakers—Maruti Suzuki and Mahindra & Mahindra (OTC:MAHMF)—as outsized beneficiaries.

Why the Auto Sector Is Ground Zero

Autos are a politically and economically visible sector. They employ millions directly and indirectly, and they sit at the edge of affordability for households. That makes them hypersensitive to tax changes.

A GST cut rewires the industry's demand curve. Small cars, once affordable entry points, were squeezed by regulatory costs and higher levies. Sport utility vehicles (SUVs), increasingly dominant in the consumer preference mix, were stuck in the punitive 28%+ tax bucket. Both could benefit under a new 18% standard rate.

In effect, autos may provide investors with the cleanest, most levered way to play GST reform. And within autos, Maruti Suzuki and Mahindra & Mahindra represent two complementary strategies—the former tied to affordability and first-time buyers, the latter to SUVs, electric vehicles (EVs) and rural farm equipment.

Maruti Suzuki: Affordability Reset

For years, Maruti has been challenged by the shrinking of India's hatchback market. From 46% of sales in FY19, hatchbacks are now just 21%.4 First-time buyer affordability has been under siege.

Yet, Maruti still has more than half of its sales tied to small cars. What was a liability potentially becomes a weapon under GST reform. A drop from 29%–31% effective taxation to 18% could reset price points by double digits. That, coupled with rate cuts and rising real wages, is a powerful catalyst for reviving the entry-level market.

Operating leverage could magnify the benefit. We have seen recently raised FY26–FY28 earnings per share (EPS) forecasts for Maruti by 5%–7% on SUV launches and softer input costs—before even baking in GST reform.5 With volumes recovering in small cars, the earnings power looks stronger still.

Timing helps. On September 3, 2025, Maruti will launch a new five-seater SUV between the Brezza and Grand Vitara.6 Deliveries could begin in the festive season—just as tax cuts are expected. It is the rare case where policy and product calendars align.

Mahindra & Mahindra: Scale and Diversification

If Maruti is the affordability reset story, Mahindra & Mahindra (M&M)could be the scale-up story.

M&M is India's SUV powerhouse. In Q1 FY26, auto revenues rose 32% YoY, with SUV volume growth guidance in the mid-to-high teens for FY26.7 Its SUVs—Scorpio, XUV700, Thar—are precisely in the tax bracket that would fall from 28% to 18%. For Mahindra, GST reform is gasoline on an already burning fire.

Unlike Maruti, Mahindra is not constrained to small cars. It straddles SUVs, EVs and farm equipment, giving it a hedge across urban aspirational demand and rural income growth.

  • EV traction. M&M's EV arm (MEAL8 + contract manufacturing) posted a positive EBITDA9 margin of 3.6% in Q1 FY26, up from 1% in the prior quarter. Crucially, ~80% of BEV10customers are new-to-Mahindra buyers. The EV business is expanding the franchise, not cannibalizing it. And with eligibility for India's production-linked incentives, policy tailwinds reinforce margin expansion.11
  • Pricing power. Average selling prices in the auto division rose 13% YoY in Q1. Even as steel costs rise, Mahindra has shown a willingness and ability to pass costs onto customers.12
  • Farm resilience. Tractor sales rose 10% YoY in Q1, and EBIT13 margins in farm equipment remain near 20%. With kharif sowing up 4% year-over-year and a strong monsoon, farm income could support continued growth.14

Together, these drivers make Mahindra less dependent on a single segment and more insulated from policy or cyclical shocks. If GST is the tide, M&M is positioned with multiple boats in the water.

Exports as an Additional Catalyst

Watch the U.S.-India track. In April 2025, Washington and New Delhi set "terms of reference" for a bilateral trade agreement, reviving the possibility of a limited deal that could restore preferences or carve out supply-chain tariff relief. Indian officials said in mid-July that talks were "progressing," yet it was reported in early August that the negotiations stalled after new U.S. tariff moves, even as both sides left the door open to salvage an agreement. If a narrow package materializes, it would layer an external tailwind atop GST reform—helpful for export lines (components, select models) and input costs.15

Both Maruti and Mahindra now see exports as a structural leg of growth.

  • Maruti: Exports grew 37% YoY in Q1 FY26, offsetting domestic weakness. The Fronx and Jimny are gaining traction in Japan, now the company's second-largest export market. With its upcoming EV rollout to 100 countries, Maruti is becoming a small-car export hub.16
  • Mahindra: Its global SUV ambitions are bolstered by platform launches slated for 2026–27. With EV margins turning positive and incentives in place, overseas markets could increasingly accept M&M as more than a domestic rural brand.17

Exports provide both companies with diversification and an additional multiplier effect when domestic demand surges under the GST reform.

The Macro Tailwinds Surrounding the Reform

GST reform isn't a solo act; it lands in the middle of a tailwind stack that's already nudging India toward a stronger consumption pulse. Households have a little more cash in their pockets from recent tax relief. Borrowing costs are easing, and if inflation cools further, the cost of money can drift lower still. Hiring is broadening beyond tech into the everyday economy, and pay packets are stretching a bit farther as prices stabilize. Out in the hinterland, a cooperative monsoon and healthy sowing season are doing what they always do—quietly underwriting rural demand.

Put together, that's not just noise around the edges; it's a tone change. This is the kind of backdrop where a price-level reset like GST doesn't merely trim the sticker, it changes the psychology of buying. First-time buyers who've been on the fence suddenly see the numbers pencil out. That's Maruti's sweet spot. At the same time, aspirational upgrades don't feel like a stretch when credit is easier and incomes feel less fragile. That's where Mahindra's SUVs and even tractors on the rural side find fresh fuel.

In short, policy is meeting momentum. GST is the spark, but the tinder (cash flow, credit, confidence and countryside) is already dry. That's why this doesn't read as a one-quarter blip. It reads like the opening chapter of the next consumption leg.

Conclusion: Two Sides of the Same Coin

GST reform could be India's most significant consumption reset in a decade. For investors, the clearest expression lies in autos.

  • Maruti Suzuki: the affordability play, where small-car elasticity meets policy-driven relief.
  • Mahindra & Mahindra: the scale-up play, riding SUVs, EV adoption and farm resilience into higher growth.

Together, they offer two complementary exposures to India's consumption surge. One revives the first-time buyer; the other monetizes aspirational upgrades and rural strength. Both reflect the potential multiplier power of tax cuts.

In 2008, the WisdomTree India Earnings Fund (EPI) was launched, and it is designed to track the total return performance of the WisdomTree India Earnings Index. This strategy seeks to include the profitable companies of India across the market capitalization spectrum, and these companies are weighted by earnings. As of August 18, 2025, both Maruti Suzuki and Mahindra & Mahindra were constituents, as is visible in figure 1.
